message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 11
Recent Green phase: No implementation change — "3 runes + 3 moonstones → 137 G" passed immediately as a triangulation test (per-type grouping already produces two separate blocks of 60).

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. No code changed this cycle; evaluate existing helpers (componentItemsBase, countByType) for any clarity/duplication improvement. If none warranted, document why. Do not add behavior for future tests (no modifiers, no claim logic).

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

      Tests  11 passed | 31 todo (42)
   Start at  18:46:22
   Duration  163ms (transform 34ms, setup 0ms, collect 27ms, tests 3ms, environment 0ms, prepare 48ms)

content.text:
All 11 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the implementation through the Simple Design lens.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Let me focus on the two helpers I was asked to evaluate, plus their neighbors:

- **`countByType`** — counts items grouped by their `type` field, returning a `Map<type, count>`. The name clearly reveals intent: it counts by type. Accurate.
- **`componentItemsBase`** — computes the total base premium across component items, applying the per-type block rule. The name reads as a noun phrase ("component items base"). It returns the base premium for component items. Reasonable, and it parallels the inline `mainBase` calculation in `quote`. Accurate but slightly terse.
- **`componentBasePremium`** — computes the base premium for `count` alike components. Clear.
- **`withFirstInsurance`** — applies the 10% first-insurance surcharge. Clear.
- **`roundUpInMHPCOFavor`** — clear, well-commented.

The names already reveal intent. No rename is warranted — renaming `countByType` or `componentItemsBase` would not increase clarity and could break the parallelism with the spec language ("alike components", "base premium").

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Focusing on the two helpers under review:

**`countByType`** (lines 38-44):
```
const counts = new Map();              // binding(counts) 1, invocation(new Map) 2
for (const item of items) {            // loop 5, binding(item) 1, binding(items) 1
  counts.set(item.type,                // invocation(set) 2, invocation(.type access ~binding) — type access: binding 1
    (counts.get(item.type) ?? 0) + 1); // invocation(get) 2, binding(item.type) 1, constant(0) 1, invocation(+) 2, constant(1) 1
}
return counts;                          // binding 1
```
Approx mass: bindings (counts, item, items, item.type, item.type, counts-return) ≈ 6, invocations (new Map, set, get, +) ≈ 4×2=8, loop 5, constants (0,1) 2 → **~21**

**`componentItemsBase`** (lines 48-54):
```
let base = 0;                                          // binding(base) 1, constant(0) 1
for (const count of countByType(componentItems).values()) {  // loop 5, binding(count) 1, invocation(countByType) 2, invocation(values) 2, binding(componentItems) 1
  base += withFirstInsurance(componentBasePremium(count));    // assignment(+=) 6, invocation(withFirstInsurance) 2, invocation(componentBasePremium) 2, binding(count) 1
}
return base;                                            // binding 1
```
Approx mass: assignment 6, loop 5, invocations 8, bindings 5, constant 1 → **~25**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 11 green. Confirmed.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Names are clear; comments explain the non-obvious "exactly one block" and "per-type" rules well. No improvement needed.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** I checked for duplication between the `mainBase` reduce in `quote` and `componentItemsBase`. Both apply `withFirstInsurance` to a per-item/per-group base. However, they differ structurally: main items are priced individually (one premium per item), while components are priced per *type group* (the block rule operates on counts). Forcing them into a shared abstraction would obscure that the two pricing models are genuinely different — that would hurt Rule 2 (clarity), which trumps Rule 3 and APP. No conceptual duplication that warrants extraction.

`componentItemsBase` uses an imperative `let base = 0; for ... base += ...` accumulator. This is a recognizable summation pattern. Expressing it as a reduce over the map values would match the style of the `mainBase` reduce in `quote` and remove the mutable accumulator (an assignment, APP mass 6). This is a Rule 2 (consistency/intent) and APP improvement without sacrificing clarity.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** No unnecessary abstractions. Each helper earns its place.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll convert the imperative accumulator in `componentItemsBase` to a reduce, matching the `mainBase` style in `quote`. This removes the `let`/`+=` mutation.

   Start at  18:46:49
   Duration  170ms (transform 29ms, setup 0ms, collect 28ms, tests 3ms, environment 0ms, prepare 50ms)

content.text:
All 11 tests still green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**`componentItemsBase`** (refactored):
```
[...countByType(componentItems).values()].reduce(   // spread/invocation(countByType) 2, invocation(values) 2, binding(componentItems) 1, invocation(reduce) 2
  (base, count) => base + withFirstInsurance(componentBasePremium(count)),  // binding(base) 1, binding(count) 1, invocation(+) 2, invocation(withFirstInsurance) 2, invocation(componentBasePremium) 2, binding(count) 1
  0,                                                  // constant 1
);
```
Approx mass: invocations (countByType, values, reduce, +, withFirstInsurance, componentBasePremium) ≈ 6×2=12, bindings (componentItems, base, count, count) ≈ 4, constant 1 → **~17**

Mass change for `componentItemsBase`: **~25 → ~17 (Δ −8)**. The drop comes from eliminating the `assignment` (`+=`, mass 6) and the standalone `loop` construct, replaced by an `invocation` (`reduce`).

`countByType` was left unchanged (mass ~21). I considered converting it to a reduce as well, but its imperative `Map.set` form is the idiomatic and clearest way to build a frequency map; a reduce returning the accumulator map would be more cryptic and hurt Rule 2. Left as-is intentionally.

## Step 6: Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Naming: Evaluated all helpers (`countByType`, `componentItemsBase`, `componentBasePremium`, `withFirstInsurance`, `roundUpInMHPCOFavor`) — all already reveal intent. No rename warranted.
- Rule 3 / consistency: Converted `componentItemsBase` from an imperative `let base = 0; for … base += …` accumulator to a `reduce`, matching the existing `mainBase` reduce style in `quote`. This removes mutable state and makes the two base-premium summations stylistically parallel.
- APP mass: `componentItemsBase` dropped ~25 → ~17 (Δ −8), eliminating one assignment and one explicit loop.

**Considered but not done (with reasons):**
- Extracting a shared abstraction between `mainBase` and `componentItemsBase`: declined. Main items price per-item; components price per-type-group (block rule). They are genuinely different pricing models — unifying them would obscure intent (Rule 2 trumps Rule 3/APP).
- Converting `countByType` to a reduce: declined. The `Map.set` frequency-map idiom is clearer than a reduce that threads the map as an accumulator (Rule 2).
- No future behavior (modifiers, claim logic) was added.

## Step 7: Report

🔄 Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Converted `componentItemsBase` from an imperative loop-with-mutable-accumulator to a `reduce`, matching the `mainBase` reduce style in `quote`. Removed one assignment and one explicit loop.
- **Mass Change**: `componentItemsBase` ~25 → ~17 (Δ −8)
- **Tests**: All 11 passing ✅

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_18-30-07_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
